clean_story:
Unplanned discoveries. Mistakes that reshaped history.

The phrase "by accident" describes outcomes no one intended—like a chef burning sugar but inventing caramel, or a scientist spilling mold on bacteria and finding penicillin. It’s the opposite of design; progress stumbling over its own feet.

Most accidents fail. A few rewrite rules. In 1945, Percy Spencer stood near a magnetron (a radar tube) and noticed his candy bar melted. Instead of dismissing it, he pointed the device at popcorn kernels. They exploded. The microwave oven was born—not from a plan, but from a curious pause. Similarly, 3M chemist Spencer Silver tried to make super-strong glue in 1968. He failed. His "weak" adhesive let paper stick and unstick without tearing—Post-it Notes arrived a decade later when another employee used it to mark hymnbook pages.

Accidents thrive where rigid goals don’t. Viagra began as a heart drug. Saccharin was discovered when a chemist forgot to wash his hands before lunch. Even the universe’s existence might be an accident: some theories suggest our Big Bang was a random quantum fluctuation in an empty void.

Three related subjects are serendipity in science, failed inventions repurposed, and chaos in complex systems.
